concepts.lesson: Drainage refers to the river system of an area. It describes how water from different streams and rivers flows and drains into larger water bodies like seas or oceans.
concepts.real_example: Small streams from hills join together to form larger rivers such as the Ganga.
key_points: Water flows towards a common outlet

concepts.lesson: A drainage basin is the area drained by a single river and its tributaries. All the water within this region eventually flows into the same river.
concepts.real_example: The Ganga basin is one of the largest drainage basins in India.
key_points: Area drained by a river system

concepts.lesson: A water divide is an elevated land area such as a hill or mountain that separates two drainage basins.
concepts.real_example: The upland near Ambala separates the Indus and Ganga river systems.

concepts.lesson: Himalayan rivers originate from glaciers and receive water from both snowmelt and rainfall. These rivers are perennial and flow throughout the year.
concepts.real_example: The Ganga, Brahmaputra and Indus are major Himalayan rivers.

concepts.lesson: Peninsular rivers originate from the Peninsular plateau and mainly depend on rainfall. Most of these rivers are seasonal.
concepts.real_example: Godavari, Krishna, Mahanadi and Kaveri are important peninsular rivers.
key_points: Originate in Western Ghats or central highlands
Flow mostly towards the Bay of Bengal

concepts.lesson: A river system consists of a main river along with all its tributaries that join it during its journey to the sea.
concepts.real_example: The Ganga river system includes tributaries like Yamuna, Ghaghara and Kosi.

concepts.lesson: A tributary is a smaller river or stream that joins a larger river during its course.
concepts.real_example: Yamuna is a major tributary of the Ganga.

concepts.lesson: A delta is formed when a river deposits sediments at its mouth where it enters a sea or ocean. Over time, these deposits form fertile land.
concepts.real_example: The Sundarban delta is formed by the Ganga and Brahmaputra rivers.

concepts.lesson: Some rivers flow through rift valleys created by tectonic activity. These valleys form due to faulting of the earth’s crust.
concepts.real_example: The Narmada and Tapi rivers flow through rift valleys.

concepts.lesson: A lake is a body of water surrounded by land. Lakes may be natural or formed by human activities such as dam construction.
concepts.real_example: Dal Lake in Kashmir attracts many tourists.

concepts.lesson: Rivers are vital for human life because they provide water for agriculture, industries, transport and electricity generation.
concepts.real_example: Many cities such as Varanasi and Kolkata developed along rivers.

concepts.lesson: River pollution occurs when harmful substances such as sewage, chemicals and industrial waste enter rivers and contaminate the water.
concepts.real_example: Untreated industrial waste released into rivers can damage aquatic life.
key_points: Caused by sewage and industrial waste
